coredata.dc:title: Direct phoxim sensing based on fluorescent metal-organic framework of Nu-1000 induced FRET
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Wenhui
Huang, Gengli
Jiang, Guoyong
Dauda, Sa-adu Abiola
Pi, Fuwei
coredata.dc:description: To control and on-site evaluate the contamination of organophosphorus pesticides, one direct sensing approach based on spontaneous fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) was developed in this study. The luminescent metal-organic frameworks (LMOFs), a novel achievement of MOFs, could provide adjustable adsorption capacity and fluorescence index to scientific analysis was first employed to evaluate phoxim molecules in vegetables and fruits. Through the π-π recognition and FRET sensing between phoxim molecules and skeleton of Nu-1000 LMOFs probes, an ultra-low detection limit of 1.65 pg/L in the range of 5×10−7 to 5×10−3 mg/mL (R 2=0.998) was achieved in the presence of phoxim. Such simple and accurate LMOFs- FRET-based sensing strategy could offer valuable reference for developing sensitive FRET sensors and/or on-site evaluation approaches in the areas of food quality control and food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nu-1000
Fluorescence
Detection
Phoxim
Sensitive
FRET

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of Nu-1000 concentration and pH values
Specificity of Nu-1000 sensing on phoxim
Sensing mechanism for direct Nu-1000 on evaluation of phoxim

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Basic characterization of Nu-1000 powder. (a) Typical SEM images of Nu-1000, inset: the actual sample of Nu-1000 powder and the details of its crystal structure; (b) Excitation (red) and emission (black) spectra of Nu-1000; (c) Infrared (IR) spectra of the Nu-1000; (d) PXRD pattern of Nu-1000; (e) XPS survey spectra of Nu-1000; (f) XPS analysis for Zr 3d of Nu-1000. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Emission spectra of Nu-1000 suspensions at 310 nm in the presence (red) and absence (black) phoxim molecules with 5×10−3 mg/mL. Inset is the corresponding picture of Nu-1000 in the presence (right) and absence (left) of phoxim under irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp; (b) Quenching efficiency ((F0–F)/F0) of Nu-1000-phoxim with different concentrations of Nu-1000; (c) Quenching efficiency of Nu-1000-phoxim at different pH values; (d) Incubation time of Nu-1000-phoxim vs FL intensity. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the Nu-1000 suspension upon the addition of various concentrations of phoxim under excitation at 310 nm; (b) The corresponding Stern–Volmer linear fitting curves of Nu-1000 versus phoxim.
Variances of the fluorescent intensity of Nu-1000 against various interfering species at 491 nm under 310 nm irradiation.

(a) Fluorescence lifetime decay profile for Nu-1000. (b) Fluorescence lifetime decay profile for Nu-1000+phoxim. (c) Excitation fluorescence spectra of Nu-1000 (black) and Ultraviolet absorption spectra of phoxim (red). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Schematic illustration of the FRET based direct Nu-1000 fluorescence sensing on phoxim molecules. Nu-1000 emits green fluorescence under excitation at 310 nm. After adding phoxim, the fluorescence is significantly quenched due to the spontaneous FRET process between Nu-1000 nanocolumn and phoxim molecules.
Comparison with other phoxim detection methods and materials.

head.ce:title: Direct phoxim sensing based on fluorescent metal-organic framework of Nu-1000 induced FRET

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, School of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi, Jiangsu, 214122, People's Republic of China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology
School of Food Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To control and on-site evaluate the contamination of organophosphorus pesticides, one direct sensing approach based on spontaneous fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) was developed in this study. The luminescent metal-organic frameworks (LMOFs), a novel achievement of MOFs, could provide adjustable adsorption capacity and fluorescence index to scientific analysis was first employed to evaluate phoxim molecules in vegetables and fruits. Through the π-π recognition and FRET sensing between phoxim molecules and skeleton of Nu-1000 LMOFs probes, an ultra-low detection limit of 1.65 pg/L in the range of 5×10−7 to 5×10−3 mg/mL (R 2=0.998) was achieved in the presence of phoxim. Such simple and accurate LMOFs- FRET-based sensing strategy could offer valuable reference for developing sensitive FRET sensors and/or on-site evaluation approaches in the areas of food quality control and food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs), currently the leading varieties of insecticides, are widely utilized in agriculture for their strong toxicity and high efficacy around the world (Chen et al., 2020). However, OPs are mainly targeted at the irreversible phosphorylation and inactivation of acetyl cholinesterase (AChE) in central and peripheral nervous systems (Fahimi-Kashani & Hormozi-Nezhad, 2016), thus, overload utilization or excessive residue could cause serious neurotoxicity to aquatic organisms, animals and being humans (Lv et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2017). Phoxim, a kind of widely employed OPs in recent years (Sun et al., 2018), poses a serious threat to the environment and human health due to its pretty long decay time in soil and extending food chain (Mali et al., 2023; Tan et al., 2022). To stop the spread of such public issue, many countries and regions have proclaimed a maximum residual of phoxim in food materials and environments. For example, the maximum residue of phoxim in fresh vegetables and fruits in the European Union has been proclaimed as < 0.01 mg/mL. Therefore, to ensure human health, effective and convenient evaluations of phoxim molecules in agricultural products and environments are very essential and important.

To date, many evaluation methods have been developed based on advanced instruments to quantify the phoxim molecules for diverse substrates and applications (Zhao et al., 2018), such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Zhang et al., 2022), ultra-high-performance liquid chromatography coupled to tandem mass spectrometry (UHPLC-MS/MS) (Tian et al., 2016). Undoubtedly, these conventional instrument-based techniques usually require complex pretreatments, expensive instruments, and skilled operations. To overcome these shortcomings and specifically detect the phoxim molecules, several of direct and high-throughput detection methods had been developed, e.g., surface-enhanced raman spectroscopy (SERS) (Ye et al., 2022; Zhai et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2019), electrochemical sensing (Huo et al., 2022; Tan et al., 2015; Wan et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2015; Zhao et al., 2020) and fluorescence evaluation (Cheng et al., 2021; Feng et al., 2018; Su et al., 2022; Zheng et al., 2018). Among these advanced and direct strategies, fluorescence sensing as a megascopic approach has been widely developed due to its high sensitivity, low sample consumption, and handy operations.

Although fluorescence approaches are being widely developed as the most promising evaluation technique for Ops (Fu et al., 2022; Lin et al., 2018), co-existing cosolvents and analogues in the system highly restrict its specificity and accuracy due to the competition and impacts on predesigned active sites. Metal-organic framework (MOF) materials, a class of multifunctional nanomaterials formed with metal nodes and organic ligands, had been studied extensively for specific enrichments and extractions based on their convenient synthesis, adjustable pore sizes, rich metal active sites and high reactive activity (Ma et al., 2020). MOF's different aperture leads to expected size selectivity, which means only the right sized molecules, can enter. Besides, the adsorption capacity of MOF materials allows a high accumulation of test substances to improve the detection sensitivity. Luminescent MOFs (LMOFs) as one of the new directions of MOFs, not only have the basic advantages of MOFs materials, but also show fluorescence features, hold great potential in the sensing of pesticides, various hazards (Cai et al., 2021; Luo et al., 2022) and other active substances (Chen et al., 2019; Gan et al., 2022) in the foods and environments (Du et al., 2021).

In this paper, a spontaneous fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) sensing strategy for on-site sensitive evaluations of phoxim molecules was first developed based on LMOFs of Nu-1000. The schematic illustration of Nu-1000 LMOFs on direct evaluation of phoxim molecules was given in Scheme 1 . Nu-1000 is a stable zirconium-based MOF nanocolumn in water and acid-base conditions (Webber et al., 2020). Attributed to the strong bonding affinity between high charged Zr (IV) metal ions and carboxylate O atoms in the TBAPy ligand (Zhang et al., 2014), Nu-1000 LMOFs display exceptional chemical stability with abundant pyrene nuclear structure and benzene rings in the skeleton (Pankajakshan et al., 2018). Nu-1000 possessed mesoporous 31 Å hexagonal channels, which allowed phoxim to enter. Then phoxim was located near the pyrene nucleus through π-π interaction. When using 391 nm light to excite, FRET occurred between pyrene nucleus and phoxim, and the fluorescence of Nu-1000 was significantly quenched. Based on the native fluorescence and strong π-π recognition, a FRET process spontaneously occurred between Nu-1000 nanocolumn and phoxim molecules for sensitive and direct evaluation of phoxim. Herein, the detailed approach and operational principle for direct sensing and spontaneous FRET was given.

Phoxim, dimethoate, chlorpyrifos, acephate, diazinon, fenthion and trichlorphon were purchased from Aladdin industrial Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 1,3,6,8-Tetra (4′-carboxyphenyl) pyrene (H4TBAPy) was supplied by Adamas Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). n, n’-dimethylformamide (DMF), acetone, zirconium oxychloride octahydrate (ZrOCl2·8H2O), hydrochloric acid (HCl), acetic acid, quinine sulfate dihydrate and benzoic acid were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the reagents were of AR grade and were used without further treatment. Ultrapure water thoroughly used during the experiments was obtained from a Millipore water purification system (18.2 MΩ, Milli-Q, Millipore, Burlington, MA, USA). Apple and celery were purchased from the local market.

Nu-1000 was synthesized according to a reported method (Islamoglu et al., 2018). Briefly, ultrasonic mixing a 0.15 mmoL ZrOCl2·8H2O, a 8.19 mmoL benzoic acid, and a 4 mL of DMF in a pyrex vial, followed by incubating the clarified solution in an oven at 100°C for 1 h. After cooling the oven to room temperature, a 0.06 mmoL H4TBAPy and a 0.26 mmoL Trifluoroacetic acid was added into the oven. Finally, the mixture was placed into a 100°C oven for 18 h. Separate the material by centrifugation and wash it three times with DMF. Suspend the obtained solid in 6 mL of DMF and add hydrochloric acid to the aqueous solution. The mixture was heated in an oven for 18 h. Then wash the centrifuged solid precipitation with DMF and acetone three times, respectively.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were acquired in a Hitachi high-resolution scanning electron microscope (SUS8100, Japan) with 15 kV accelerating voltage. The UV–vis spectra at 1 nm interval were obtained on a T9 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., China). The crystal structure of Nu-1000 was recorded on a D2 PHASER X-ray diffractometer (Karlsruhe, Germany) through using Cu-Kα radiation at 30 kV and 10 mA as the X-ray source. Fluorescence spectra were observed on a Fluoro Max4 (Horiba JY, Irvine, CA, USA). The fluorescence lifetime was measured using a steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS1000, UK), meanwhile, a picosecond pulsed laser (EPL) with the excitation wavelength of 373 nm was used for photon counting. The monitoring wavelength of 491 nm and the time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technology was used to count emission photons.

A 3.00 mg of Nu-1000 powder was dissolved in 2.00 mL of methanol with ultrasonication for 5 min. To confirm the sensing features of Nu-1000, series of standard solution of phoxim from 5×10−6 to 5×10−2 mg/mL were prepared, and then a 100.00 μL of standard solution and a 30.00 μL of Nu-1000 solution were well mixed and constant poured into 1.00 mL of ultrapure water (pH=5.5). Mix the above reagents well. Then, the fluorescence intensities were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 391 nm after a stationary reaction for 5 min at room temperature (25°C). The fluorescence intensities of the standard solution were recorded at 491 nm.

Apple pulp was centrifuged (8000 rpm, 4°C, 15 min), and the obtained supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane, the collection was then diluted 100 times with ultrapure water. Three phoxim solutions (5 μg/mL, 3 μg/mL, 0.5 μg/mL) with predetermined concentrations were randomly added into the diluted solutions for spiked recovery experiments. All experiments were repeated three times. The treatments of celery were same as apple pulp.

The morphology and molecular structure of as-prepared Nu-1000 powder was characterized prior to further application. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the Nu-1000 crystal particles display a very uniform size and regular hexagonal prism shape as recorded in SEM images. Meanwhile, the solid powder has a pale-yellow color and a mean size of ca. 1.32 μm in length (Fig. 1a). Recorded excitation and emission spectra showed that the as-prepared Nu-1000 powder o1btained the best emission at 491 nm under an excitation of 310 nm (Fig. 1b). Attributed to the stacked π-π* transition of pyrene cores in Nu-1000 (Zhou et al., 2018), the UV absorption diagram of as-prepared Nu-1000 powder showed a distinctive absorption peak between 257 nm and 350 nm (Fig. S1), which corresponds to its fluorescence excitation spectrum.

To further confirm the crystal structure of as-prepared Nu-1000 powder, the FT-IR spectroscopy and X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of Nu-1000 were also measured. As plotted in Fig. 1c and d, triplet characteristic peaks at 782, 716, and 664 cm−1 corresponding to the Zr–O longitudinal and transverse mode vibrations, respectively, are clearly present in the obtained FT-IR spectrum. Furthermore, two sharp and intense peaks at 1604 and 1420 cm−1 ascribed to in- and out-of-plane stretching of carboxylate groups in the liganded backbone of Nu-1000 could be easily observed. In addition to the characteristic peaks ascribed to bonded Zr–O and carboxylate groups, the XRD pattern also confirmed that our as-prepared Nu-1000 powder has classical hexagonal structure (space group: P6/mmm). The reproduced XRD pattern indicates sharp peaks at 2.5°, 5.1°, 7.3°, and 10.3° which correspond (010), (020), (021), and (040) crystal planes, respectively. These observations from FT-IR spectroscopy and XRD patterns are fully consistent with previous reports (Biswas et al., 2020). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectra demonstrate that Zr 3d, O 1s, and C 1s are present in the Nu-1000 (Fig. 1e). Fig. 1f shows high-resolution scans of Zr 3d binding energies. Nu-1000 exhibits characteristic Zr 3d peaks located at 185.3 and 183.0 eV which indicate the existence of Zr4+. These further confirms the regular structure of as-prepared Nu-1000.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Nu-1000 concentration and pH values
ce:section.ce:para:
Due to the high-performance on fluorescence quenching of phoxim to Nu-1000 (Fig. 2 a). The optimized ratio of Nu-1000 powder to phoxim was decided based on the following equation:
(1)
Q =
(
F 0
− F
)
/
F 0

Here, Q was the fluorescence quenching efficiency. F
0 was the fluorescence intensity of reference, i.e., the solution of pure Nu-1000 with constant a weight, and F was the fluorescence intensity of the sample with phoxim added.

According to such calculation, when the concentration of phoxim was maintained at 5×10−4 mg/mL, it could be easily observed that the quenching rate gradually decreased with increasing Nu-1000 (Fig. 2b). Obviously, an excessive probe would reduce the detection sensitivity. Therefore, a 30 μL of Nu-1000 was selected as the detection concentration of phoxim, i.e., 1.5 mg/mL.

Similarly, as illustrated in Fig. 2c, the optimized pH for the sensing operation system was selected at 5.5. The preparation and evaluation process of pH optimization is as follows: firstly, mixing the Nu-1000 powder with ultrapure water with pre-determined pH values, i.e., 3.5, 4.5, 5.5, 6.5, 7.5, 8.5, 9.5, at same concentration, respectively. The concentration of phoxim was maintained at 5×10−4 mg/mL in each sample. Then, the fluorescence intensities were sequentially recorded after a stationary reaction for 5 min at room temperature. The solution without phoxim was used as reference.

Reaction time was an important factor during the practical applications of pesticide detection. Rapid sensing response would greatly improve the detection efficiency. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of a detection system with phoxim at 5×10−4 mg/mL had been tracked for 50 min. As shown in Fig. 2d, phoxim molecules could rapidly complete the fluorescence quenching process on Nu-1000 and reach stability in 5 min, which indicates that our developed sensing strategy on phoxim had a great potential to be applied in rapid detection.

To further confirm the high-performance on fluorescence sensing of phoxim with Nu-1000, the quantitative detection of phoxim was carried out under optimized conditions. As plotted in Fig. 3 a, it could be clearly observed that the fluorescence emission at 491 nm progressively decreased along with the addition of phoxim into the aqueous solution of Nu-1000. Based on the fluorescence measurements, a calibration curve was obtained for the quantitative evaluation of phoxim based on Nu-1000. As plotted in Fig. 3b, the phoxim could be linearly detected in the concentration range from 5×10−7 mg/mL to 5×10−3 mg/mL: a good linear correlation with R2=0.998 and a low limit of detection (LOD) value of 1.65 pg/L, which is much lower than the maximum allowable concentration of phoxim in China and the European Union, i.e., 0.01 mg/kg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of Nu-1000 sensing on phoxim
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the specificity of Nu-1000 on sensing phoxim, other commonly used organophosphorus pesticides and commonly coexistent interfering ions were selected, i.e., dimethoate, chlorpyrifos, acephate, diazinon, fenthion, trichlorphon, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, NO3 −, SO3 2− and SO4 2− ions. All of the chemicals and ions were kept at 10 fold concentrations than phoxim in the system for quantitative analysis; meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of the detection system was determined under 310 nm excitation.

As indicated in Fig. 4 , which plotted the interference performance, only phoxim molecules significantly quenched the fluorescence intensity of Nu-1000 compared with other common pesticides and ions. As its UV spectrum (Fig. S2) showed that the absorption peak of phoxim was different from other organophosphorus pesticides in the range of 250–320 nm suggested that only phoxim could cause obvious quenching fluorescence, which was consistent with the results in Fig. 4. So, this probe supported its high potential in accurate detection.

To evaluate the sensing performance of Nu-1000 probe in complex food systems, celery and apple were selected as contaminated references for real system detection. Three standard phoxim solutions with pre-determined concentrations were randomly added into the home-made juices for recovery experiments on phoxim. Prior to the fluorescence measurements, prepared actual sample solutions were diluted by 100 times and the pH value was adjusted to 5.5. Calculated recovery rate of phoxim in celery juice is ranged from 93.90% to 98.34% with a relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 5%, and the recovery rate of phoxim in apple juice is ranged from 91.98% to 97.32% with an RSD less than 5% in the current study. Detailed recovery results are shown in Table S1.

Such acceptable results implied that the application of Nu-1000 probe in directly phoxim sensing had a bright prospect in real samples. Furthermore, based on the comparisons of this work with other previous methods on phoxim evaluations, our as-prepared Nu-1000 strategy is more sensitive than lots of the latest reports, e.g., mass spectroscopy, UV, Raman spectroscopy and fluorescence methods, except for the innately super-sensitive electrochemical technique. A detailed comparison between our work and previous reports on phoxim was summarized in Table 1 . Obviously, such comparison and direct evaluation in the food system have solidly confirmed the high-efficiency sensing capability of the Nu-1000 probe on phoxim.

To ensure the reproducibility of Nu-1000 towards phoxim, the stability of Nu-1000 was tested. Take 30 μL of Nu-1000 solution (1.5 mg/mL, methanol), constant poured into 1.00 mL of ultrapure water (pH=5.5), and measure the material by using a fluorescence spectrometer at the optimal excitation wavelength (λex=310 nm) for 7 months. The result (Fig. S3) showed that the Nu-1000 probe could remain stable, indicating that the Nu-1000 probe had high fluorescence stability. The Nu-1000 solid can be stored for more than 7 months under 4°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism for direct Nu-1000 on evaluation of phoxim
ce:section.ce:para: As we all know, fluorescence quenching is usually divided into two types, i.e., static quenching and dynamic quenching. Generally, in the static quenching process, the fluorescent group and quenching agent usually form as a complex. The formation of such a complex would hardly cause changes in the fluorescent life of the group. Conversely, the significant characteristic of dynamic quenching was that the fluorescent life of fluorescent groups usually changed more than 1 ns (Ghosh et al., 2021). To clarify the fluorescence sensing mechanism of Nu-1000 direct on phoxim, the fluorescent life of Nu-1000 in detection system in case of the phoxim (0.01 mg/mL) existence and no existence and the molecular distance between Nu-1000 and phoxim in the detection system was calculated, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescent life of our as-prepared Nu-1000 was measured at 5.40 ns. However, when it was exposed to phoxim, the fluorescent life was dropped into 3.12 ns (Fig. 5b). Therefore, it was inferred that quenching of Nu-1000-phoxim was dynamic quenching. To gain further insight into the mechanism of quenching, the absorption spectrum of phoxim and fluorescence spectra of Nu-1000 were collected and shown in Fig. 5c. Significant spectral overlap between the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of phoxim and the excitation spectrum of Nu-1000 suggested that the quenching may be a phenomenon from fluorescent resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Zhang et al., 2018). For a FRET process, the key requirements should be that: the excitation spectrum of fluorescent groups and the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of quenching agent are overlapping, fluorescent life of the system should change, and the molecular distance between the fluorescent group and quenching agent is less than 10 nm.

Undoubtedly, the first two factors have been confirmed, as illustrated in Fig. 5, i.e., the shortening of fluorescent life (Fig. 5a and b) and peak overlapping (Fig. 5c). Furthermore, according to the Förster theory (Chen et al., 2014; Yuan et al., 2017), the energy transfer efficiency of FRET can be obtained by the following Eq. (2):
(2)
E =
R 0 6
R 0 6
+
R 6
where R
0 is the Förster distance; R is the distance between the energy donor and acceptor. For our Nu-1000 fluorescence system, the energy transfer efficiency (E) could be expressed with Eq. (3):
(3)
E = 1 −
F
F 0
where F and F
0 are the fluorescence intensities of donor in the presence and absence of accepter in Fig. 2a, respectively. Therefore, the transfer efficiency of Nu-1000 was obtained as E = 50%, and the Förster distance R
0 could be calculated with Eq. (4):
(4)
R 0 6
= 8.79 ×
10
− 25
K 2
n
− 4
Φ J
where k
2 is the dipole orientation factor (k2= 2/3 for random orientation as in fluid solution), n is the refractive index of the medium (nH2O=1.333), and Φ is the quantum yield of the donor, and J is the spectral overlap integral between the normalized donor emission and the acceptor extinction coefficient, which is given as Eq. (5):
(5)
J =
∑ F
( λ )
ε
( λ )
λ 4
Δ λ
∑ F
( λ )
Δ λ
Where λ is the wavelength, F(λ) is the corrected fluorescence intensity of the donor in the wavelength range from λ to (λ+Δλ) with the total intensity normalized to unity, and ε(λ) is the acceptor molar absorption coefficient at λ. In the current work, the quinoline sulfate in 0.1 M sulfuric acid was employed as standard product for the calculation of relative quantum yields. Detailed calculations for the quantum yield of Nu-1000, i.e., Φ = 1.12%, were given in the Supporting Information.

Following the measured spectra and above calculations, the spectral overlap integral (J) was calculated to be 3.315×10−19 cm3·L·moL−1 according to Eq. (5) in Fig. S4. Then R0 was calculated to be 0.30 nm by Eq. (4). The energy transfer efficiency was calculated to be 84.16% by Eq. (3). The distance between them was subsequently calculated to be 0.25 nm by Eq. (2), which was short enough to allow an efficient FRET. On the other side, according to the confirmed structure of Nu-1000 and the characteristic band at 312 nm which is attributed to the π-π* transition of pyrene core in Nu-1000 (Stylianou et al., 2010; Zhou et al., 2018), it is reasonable to concluded that, in our direct phoxim sensing, the phoxim molecules probably are first captured by aromatic backbone of Nu-1000 (Bondžić et al., 2022; Yabushita et al., 2016), subsequently, under the π-π interactions between phoxim molecules and pyrene cores, a semi-quantitative FRET quenching occurs and facilitates the sensitive evaluation on phoxim.

In summary, by employing LMOFs of Nu-1000 as an extracted matrix, the current work has developed a spontaneous FRET sensing strategy for on-site direct evaluations of phoxim molecules. Through the FRET sensing and π-π recognition between phoxim molecules and Nu-1000 probes, the fluorescence of Nu-1000 nanocolumn was effectively quenched and offered an ultra-low detection limit of 1.65 pg/L in the presence of phoxim, which is only after the latest fluorescence microarrays. Besides, phoxim can be specifically distinguished from other commonly used OPs pesticides. In view of these findings, this simple and accurate FRET-LMOFs-based sensing method paves new direction for constructing direct FRET sensors and on-site evaluations on phoxim and other food hazards, which is only after the latest fluorescence microarrays.
Wenhui Hao: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft.
Gengli Huang: Writing - reviewing and editing, Supervision, Methodology.
Guoyong Jiang: Writing - reviewing and editing, Supervision.
Sa-adu Abiola Dauda: Writing - reviewing and editing.
Fuwei Pi: Writing - reviewing and editing, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fbio.2023.102967.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Biswas et al., 2020

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polypyrrole merged zirconium-based metal-organic framework Nu-1000 for detection of levodopa
Mikrochimica Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Biswas, S., Chen, Y., Xie, Y., Sun, X., & Wang, Y. (2020). Polypyrrole merged zirconium-based metal-organic framework Nu-1000 for detection of levodopa. Mikrochimica Acta, 187, 661.

ACS Applied Nano Materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bondzic, A. M., Lazarevic Pasti, T. D., Pasti, I. A., Bondzic, B. P., Momcilovic, M. D., Loosen, A., & Parac-Vogt, T. N. (2022). Synergistic effect of sorption and hydrolysis by Nu-1000 nanostructures for removal and detoxification of chlorpyrifos. ACS Applied Nano Materials, 5, 3312-3324.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Cai et al., 2021

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Capsulation of AuNCs with AIE effect into metal-organic framework for the marriage of a fluorescence and colorimetric biosensor to detect organophosphorus pesticides
